metadata.title: Agentic Research Course
metadata.author: Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.
metadata.description: Week 2: Setting Up Claude Code for Research -- From Installation to Your First AI-Assisted Task

elements.content: Week 2: Setting Up Claude Code for Research

**Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.**
Assistant Project Scientist, Swartz Center for Computational Neuroscience
UC San Diego

[Open Science Collective](https://osc.earth)

slides.speakerNotes: Welcome back and welcome to newcomers. Last week we covered the foundation: git, GitHub, and the command line. Today we install the tool that makes the safety net necessary. If you missed Week 1, the recording is on YouTube; the link is in the Discord. The course page and Discord are linked on the slide.

elements.content: For those who missed last week, or need a refresher:

items.text: **git** = version control on your machine. Commits = snapshots you can return to
**GitHub** = online platform. PRs, code review, issues
**Terminal** = text interface. Where Claude Code lives
**`gh` CLI** = GitHub from the command line

elements.code:
# git: version control        # gh: GitHub from CLI
git add .                     gh issue create
git commit -m "update"        gh pr create
git push                      gh repo clone user/repo
git log                       gh issue develop 42

elements.content: Week 1 recording + setup guide available in the Discord and on YouTube

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 6x to reveal all fragments]

Our audience grew 10x since last week, so let me give a 2-minute foundation. Git tracks every change to your files. GitHub stores them online and enables collaboration. The terminal is the text interface where Claude Code runs. And gh is the GitHub CLI tool. The key insight from Week 1: AI is powerful, so version control and quality assurance matter more, not less.

elements.alt: Claude face icon with wink animation

elements.content: An agentic coding tool that reads, writes, runs, and iterates

items.text: Reads your **entire project** and understands the structure
Writes and edits code **across multiple files**
Runs terminal commands (build, test, lint, deploy)
Creates git commits, branches, and pull requests
Plans multi-step implementations **before writing a line**
Iterates on its own work until tests pass

elements.content: You describe what you want. Claude Code figures out how to build it.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 7x to reveal all fragments]

Claude Code is fundamentally different from autocomplete or chat. It is an autonomous agent. You give it a task, and it reads your project, plans an approach, writes code across multiple files, runs tests, and iterates. You describe the outcome; it handles the implementation. This changes how you work.

elements.content: This course focuses on **Claude Code**. Cowork is new (research preview) and handles non-coding tasks like spreadsheets and presentations.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 2x to reveal all fragments]

Anthropic has three products now. Claude Chat is what most people know: conversational AI in the browser. Claude Code is the coding agent we're learning. And Cowork is brand new, a research preview that brings agentic capabilities to knowledge work: creating spreadsheets, presentations, documents. All three are available on Pro and above. We focus on Code.

elements.alt: Claude Code interface diagram showing Terminal, VS Code, Desktop, Web, JetBrains

items.text: **Terminal CLI** -- full-featured, what we teach in this course
**VS Code / Cursor** -- inline diffs, @-mentions, plan review
**Desktop App** -- visual diffs, multiple sessions, scheduled tasks
**Web** (claude.ai/code) -- no local setup, long-running tasks
**JetBrains** -- PyCharm, IntelliJ, WebStorm
**Third-party: Conductor** -- GUI wrapper, parallel agents, visual workflows

elements.content: All official surfaces share the same engine. Your CLAUDE.md, settings, and MCP servers work everywhere.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 7x to reveal all fragments]

Claude Code runs everywhere. The terminal CLI is the most complete and what we'll use. VS Code and JetBrains have extensions. There's a standalone desktop app and a web version. And Conductor is a third-party Mac app that adds a visual GUI with parallel agents. All the official surfaces share the same engine, so your configuration carries across.

Here's what it looks like in practice. On the left, the VS Code extension with inline diffs and conversation panel. On the right, the standalone desktop app with visual diff review. Both connect to the same Claude Code engine. Pick whatever feels comfortable.

elements.code:
# macOS / Linux (recommended)
curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash

# macOS via Homebrew
brew install --cask claude-code

# Windows PowerShell
irm https://claude.ai/install.ps1 | iex

elements.content: Then run `cd your-project && claude` to start. Native install auto-updates. Homebrew/WinGet require manual upgrade.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 2x to reveal all fragments]

Installation is one command. The native install is recommended because it auto-updates. Homebrew works but you need to upgrade manually. On Windows, use PowerShell. After installing, cd into any project and type claude. First time, it opens a browser for login. That's it.

elements.title: Shift+Tab
elements.content: Press **Shift+Tab** to cycle between modes anytime during a session. Start with Default, graduate to Accept Edits as you build trust.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 2x to reveal all fragments]

Claude Code has a permission model. By default it asks before writing files or running commands. You control the guardrails. Shift+Tab cycles between modes. Start with Default mode. As you build trust, move to Accept Edits. Plan mode is read-only, perfect for exploration. Auto mode is for power users, a background classifier decides what's safe.

elements.content: The most important file in your project
A markdown file with **persistent instructions** that Claude reads at the start of **every session**.

items.text: You write it; it guides Claude's behavior
A good CLAUDE.md turns a generic AI into a **domain-aware research assistant**
Think of it as the briefing you'd give a new team member
Run `/init` to generate a starter, then refine

elements.alt: Screenshot of /init generating a CLAUDE.md file

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 5x to reveal all fragments]

CLAUDE.md is the most important file for AI-assisted development. It is a plain markdown file at your project root. Every time Claude Code starts a session, it reads this file first. Your coding standards, architectural decisions, workflow conventions, and never-do rules go here. Run /init and Claude analyzes your codebase and generates a starting point. Then you refine it.

items.text: **Project CLAUDE.md** = team standards, check into git. **User** = personal, all projects.
**Local** (CLAUDE.local.md) = personal project-specific, gitignored. Subdirectory files load on demand.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 3x to reveal all fragments]

CLAUDE.md files can live at multiple levels. The project-level one goes in your repo root and is shared with your team via git. Your personal one goes in your home directory. And CLAUDE.local.md is for personal project-specific notes that you gitignore. Claude loads all of them, with more specific ones taking precedence.

elements.content: ## What Goes In vs. What Stays Out

items.text: Build and test commands
Code style rules (non-default)
Repo etiquette (branch naming, PR conventions)
Architecture decisions
Common gotchas
"Never do this" rules
Things Claude can infer from code
Standard language conventions
Detailed API docs (link instead)
Info that changes frequently
Long tutorials
Self-evident practices

elements.content: **Keep under 200 lines.** Bloated CLAUDE.md files cause Claude to ignore your actual instructions. If Claude follows a rule without the instruction, delete it.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 13x to reveal all fragments]

This is from the official Anthropic best practices. Include things Claude cannot figure out by reading your code. Exclude things it already knows. The critical rule: keep it under 200 lines. If your CLAUDE.md is too long, Claude starts ignoring rules. Prune regularly. If Claude already does something correctly without the instruction, remove it.

elements.content: For larger projects, split instructions into topic files:

elements.code:
.claude/
├── CLAUDE.md              # Main instructions
└── rules/
    ├── code-style.md      # Code style guidelines
    ├── testing.md          # Testing conventions
    └── security.md         # Security requirements

elements.content: Rules can be **path-scoped** with YAML frontmatter:

elements.code:
---
paths:
  - "src/api/**/*.ts"
---
# API Rules
- All endpoints must validate input
- Use standard error response format

elements.content: Path-scoped rules only load when Claude works with matching files, saving context space.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 4x to reveal all fragments]

When CLAUDE.md gets too long, split rules into separate files under .claude/rules/. Each file covers one topic. The powerful feature: path-scoped rules. Add YAML frontmatter with paths, and the rules only load when Claude works with matching files. This keeps context clean. We'll see this more in our research-skills templates.

elements.content: If your repo uses AGENTS.md for other tools (Copilot, Cursor), import it:

elements.code:
# CLAUDE.md
@AGENTS.md

## Claude Code Specific
Use plan mode for changes under src/billing/.

elements.content: The `@path` syntax imports files inline. Works with any file: `@README.md`, `@package.json`, `@docs/conventions.md`

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 4x to reveal all fragments]

Quick note on AGENTS.md. Some repos use it for GitHub Copilot or Cursor. Claude Code ignores it. But you can import it into your CLAUDE.md with the @ syntax. This way both tools read the same instructions without duplication. The @ import works with any file.

elements.content: Plugins, Skills, MCP, Hooks, and Agents

elements.alt: Plugin ecosystem: Plugin contains Skills, Hooks, Agents, MCP. Below: CLAUDE.md and Auto Memory always loaded.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 1x to reveal all fragments]

Here's how the pieces fit together. A Plugin is a distributable package that bundles everything. Inside a plugin you can have Skills, which are workflows Claude loads on demand. Hooks are automated actions that fire at lifecycle points, like running a linter after every file edit. Agents are isolated workers with their own context window. And MCP servers connect Claude to external tools. Below all of these, CLAUDE.md and auto-memory are always loaded.

items.text: Stored in `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`
Claude loads them **on demand**, not every session
Invoke with `/skill-name` or Claude auto-detects from context
Can include supporting files: templates, scripts, examples

rows: Run a prompt repeatedly on an interval

elements.content: **Skill vs CLAUDE.md:** CLAUDE.md = facts loaded every session. Skills = procedures loaded on demand. If CLAUDE.md is getting long, move procedures to skills.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 7x to reveal all fragments]

Skills are one of the most useful features. They are workflows stored as SKILL.md files. Unlike CLAUDE.md which loads every session, skills load only when needed. You invoke them with slash commands like /simplify or /debug. Claude can also auto-detect when a skill is relevant. The bundled skills ship with Claude Code. You can create your own.

elements.content: **Model Context Protocol** -- an open standard for connecting AI to external data sources.

elements.code:
# Add an MCP server
claude mcp add notion-server npx @notionhq/mcp-server

# Or configure in .mcp.json

items.text: **Google Drive:** read design docs, meeting notes
**Jira / Linear:** update tickets, track issues
**Databases:** query and analyze data
**Slack:** read channels, post updates
**MATLAB (matlab-mcp-tools):** run EEGLAB from Claude Code

elements.content: Our practicum uses **matlab-mcp-tools** to drive EEGLAB from Claude Code for EEG analysis.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 8x to reveal all fragments]

MCP is Model Context Protocol, an open standard. It connects Claude Code to external tools. You can read Google Drive docs, update Jira tickets, query databases, post to Slack. For our practicum, we use matlab-mcp-tools to drive EEGLAB from Claude Code. Claude runs MATLAB commands, reads EEG data, and generates figures, all from the terminal.

elements.content: Automated actions at lifecycle points. **Deterministic**, not advisory.

items.text: Run linter after every file edit
Block destructive commands
Auto-format code on save
Inject context at session start

elements.content: Isolated workers with their **own context window**.

items.text: Keeps main conversation clean
Good for research / exploration
Can restrict tools and model
Returns summary, not raw data

elements.content: **Hooks:** "This WILL happen." | **CLAUDE.md:** "Please do this." | **Agents:** "Do this in your own space."

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 9x to reveal all fragments]

Two more concepts. Hooks are automated actions that fire at specific points. They are deterministic: unlike CLAUDE.md which says please do this, hooks guarantee it happens. Run a linter after every edit, block dangerous commands, inject context at startup. Agents, or subagents, are isolated workers. They run in their own context window, so they don't clutter your main conversation. Perfect for research tasks that read many files.

elements.content: ## Plan Mode: Think Before You Code

elements.alt: Plan mode workflow: Explore, Plan, Implement, Commit

items.text: **Explore** (Plan Mode): Claude reads files, builds understanding
**Plan** (Plan Mode): Claude proposes approach, you review
**Implement** (Normal Mode): Claude writes the code
**Commit** (Normal Mode): Claude commits and opens PR

elements.content: Enter with **Shift+Tab** (twice) or `/plan`. Press **Ctrl+G** to edit the plan in your editor.

elements.alt: Screenshot of Claude Code in plan mode

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 5x to reveal all fragments]

Plan mode is one of the most important features. Press Shift+Tab twice to enter it. Claude switches to read-only: it can explore your codebase but cannot make changes. It builds understanding, then proposes a plan. You review and edit the plan, press Ctrl+G to open it in your editor. Then switch back to normal mode and Claude implements it. This prevents solving the wrong problem.

rows: Generate starter CLAUDE.md from your codebase

elements.title: Key shortcuts
elements.content: **Shift+Tab** = cycle permission modes | **Esc** = stop Claude | **Esc+Esc** = rewind | **Ctrl+G** = open in editor | **@** = file autocomplete | **!** = bash mode

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 2x to reveal all fragments]

Here are the commands you'll use most. /init to bootstrap CLAUDE.md. /plan for exploration. /clear between tasks, this is critical for keeping context clean. /compact to summarize when context fills up. /model to switch between Opus for hard problems and Sonnet for everyday tasks. And the shortcuts: Shift+Tab to cycle modes, Esc to stop, double-Esc to rewind, @ for file autocomplete.

elements.content: ### 1. Give Claude a way to verify its work
Include tests, expected outputs, or screenshots. This is the **single highest-leverage** thing you can do.
### 2. Explore first, then plan, then code
Separate research from implementation. Use Plan Mode for the thinking phase.
`/clear` between tasks. Use subagents for investigation. After two failed corrections, start fresh with a better prompt.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 6x to reveal all fragments]

These three patterns come directly from Anthropic's official best practices page. First: always give Claude a way to verify. Include tests, expected outputs, screenshots. Second: don't jump to coding. Explore first in plan mode, create a plan, then implement. Third: manage context aggressively. Use /clear between tasks. Use subagents for research. If you've corrected Claude twice and it's still wrong, /clear and write a better prompt.

elements.content: Opinionated additions on top of official Claude Code features
Four files that track your project's evolving state across sessions:
Claude reads these files. Your `plan.md` becomes its task list. Your `scratch_history.md` prevents it from repeating your mistakes.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 2x to reveal all fragments]

This is our opinionated addition, not an official Claude Code feature. The .context/ directory has four files that track project state. plan.md is your task list. ideas.md records design decisions. research.md captures technical findings. And scratch_history.md, maybe the most valuable, records failed attempts so neither you nor the AI repeat them. Claude reads all of these.

elements.content: Free, open-source Claude Code plugin for academic research workflows:

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 2x to reveal all fragments]

The research-skills plugin is the companion to this course. It's free and open source. Seven domains, each mapping to a course week. The project plugin gives you /init-project which scaffolds CLAUDE.md, .context/, .rules/ directories. opencite does literature search. grant handles proposal writing and review. And so on. We'll use these throughout the course.

items.text: Most US universities are **501(c)(3) nonprofits** -- labs and departments qualify
Nonprofit verification through Goodstack (~3 minutes)
**Education plans** available for university-wide deployment; contact Anthropic
For this course: **Pro ($20/mo)** is sufficient

elements.content: PIs: if your university is a 501(c)(3), your lab can get Team seats at **$8/seat/month** instead of $25. Verify at Goodstack in 3 minutes.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 6x to reveal all fragments]

Let's talk pricing. Pro at 20 dollars a month is enough for this course. But here's the big news for PIs and department heads: most US universities are 501(c)(3) nonprofits. That means you qualify for the nonprofit Team plan at 8 dollars per seat per month instead of 25. Premium seats drop from 125 to about 40. Verification through Goodstack takes 3 minutes. There are also university-wide education plans; contact Anthropic's education team for those.

items.text: Install Claude Code and verify it works (`claude --version`)
Run `/init` in a project to create your first CLAUDE.md
Create the `.context/` directory with `plan.md`, `ideas.md`, `research.md`, `scratch_history.md`
Ask Claude Code to do one task related to your research
Review what it generated and refine the CLAUDE.md manually
(Optional) Install the research-skills plugin

elements.content: **Next week:** Project management with AI -- epics, sprints, worktrees, and structured development workflows.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press -> 7x to reveal all fragments]

Here's your homework. Install Claude Code. Run /init to generate a CLAUDE.md. Create the .context/ directory structure. Then give Claude one real task from your research. It doesn't have to be big. Analyze a file, plot some data, write a function. See how it works. Then look at the CLAUDE.md and refine it. Next week we'll cover project management: epics, sprints, and git worktrees.

items: **Course Discord:** link in the YouTube description

slides.speakerNotes: Here are all the links. The official docs at code.claude.com are excellent. The best practices page is worth reading cover to cover. The research-skills plugin and matlab-mcp-tools are both free and open source. And if you're a PI, check the nonprofit pricing page.
Thank you. Let's open it up for questions. Feel free to type in the chat or raise your hand on the Discord stage.
